focus_areas: Relay git subprocess / spool storage / download / quotas
HTML normalizer over untrusted fetched content
Noise tunnel, framing & server identity key
Decoy HTTPS — static serving, symlink, X-Forwarded-For

findings.title: Server Noise identity key fails open: missing identity file silently generates a fresh keypair
findings.description: load_or_generate_static_key reads the identity file; on ErrorKind::NotFound (line 487) it does not error but generates a brand-new Noise static keypair, writes it to disk, logs it as a 'debug' keypair, and returns the new private key. This is the long-term static identity clients pin (impersonation root). Called unconditionally at startup from run_proxy_listener (noise.rs:41) with no production guard. If the identity file is absent (deleted, ephemeral volume, wrong cwd, failed mount, restore-without-key) the server comes up with a different public key. Impact: (1) availability/pinning break — every enrolled client fails the IK handshake against the new static and the proxy is silently unreachable to its roster; (2) defeats the multi-server-failover guarantee that nodes share the same enrolled keys — a regenerated node diverges from the fleet. No integrity check that the loaded/generated key matches an expected public key.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operator redeploys a node onto a fresh volume without restoring identity.key. Server boots, mints a new keypair, logs success, serves. Every client pinning the original static now fails the Noise IK handshake with no obvious server-side error (logged 'generated debug keypair' at info). In a censored-region deployment this is a silent total outage; in failover the node no longer matches the fleet identity so priority fallback to it always fails.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed in production: return an error on NotFound rather than generating. Gate auto-generation behind an explicit opt-in (e.g. a --generate-identity subcommand or GHOST_PROXY_DEV env) used only for first-time provisioning. Support an expected-public-key pin in config and refuse to start if the loaded key's derived public key does not match.
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed at noise.rs:487-505; matches THREAT_MODEL T4 and the owner's recorded §6 decision that a missing identity file must be a hard startup error.

findings.title: HTTP redirect targets are never re-validated against the SSRF guard
findings.description: The reqwest client is built with redirect::Policy::limited(5) (line 128) and follows up to 5 redirects automatically. The SSRF guard validate_public_url -> resolve_guard is applied only to the client-supplied original URL in submit_web (227), run_web_fetch (525), and run_package on the resolved URL (587). reqwest then performs the GET in http_get_bytes (609) and silently follows any Location header with no per-hop resolve_guard. The redirect Location is attacker/upstream-controlled. Data flow: client URL -> validate_public_url passes (attacker host is public) -> http.get().send() -> 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/... -> reqwest fetches internal/metadata host -> body spooled and returned to client via download op.
findings.exploit_scenario: An authenticated client submits web_fetch for http://attacker.com/redir. The guard resolves attacker.com to a public IP and passes. attacker.com responds 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/. reqwest follows it, fetches cloud-metadata credentials, stores them as the job body, which the client downloads. Same reaches http://127.0.0.1 internal services.
findings.recommendation: Use redirect::Policy::custom(...) and invoke resolve_guard on every intermediate URL, rejecting (attempt.error/attempt.stop) any hop resolving to a disallowed address; pin the vetted IP into the connection.
findings.confidence_reason: Directly confirms THREAT_MODEL T1; redirect policy and the single-URL-only guard are both visible in source. reqwest has no built-in SSRF protection.

findings.title: Signed SPA region omits server identity, enabling cross-server replay in the failover fleet
findings.description: The signature is computed (build_with_padding, line 107) and verified (verify, line 150) over exactly payload[..SPA_SIGNED_LEN] = version(2)+flags(1)+key_id(8)+timestamp(8)+counter(8)+nonce(16) = 43 bytes. No server/endpoint identity (e.g. the server's Noise static pubkey or a node ID) is in the authenticated bytes. Deployment is multi-server failover where nodes share the same enrolled client keys, and counter state is per-server-instance (ghost-proxy-server/src/spa.rs:16-23,125-134). A valid SPA accepted by node A, captured on the wire, is a cryptographically valid SPA for node B whose counter has not yet consumed it: signature verifies, timestamp within window, B's per-key counter is lower, so B accepts and opens its gate. This is T2.
findings.exploit_scenario: On-path attacker captures a client's SPA to node A (counter=5). A consumes 5. Before the +/-300s window closes, attacker replays the bytes to node B (which has seen counter <=4 for that key). Combined with UDP source-IP spoofing, the attacker has B add an attacker-chosen IP to B's allow-map, exposing the concealed proxy port on B and defeating concealment.
findings.recommendation: Include the server's identity (Noise static public key or a unique per-node id) in the signed region. Update SPA_SIGNED_LEN, build_with_padding, verify, and the XDP structural pre-filter together; reject any SPA whose embedded server identity does not match the local node.
findings.confidence_reason: Signed-length math verified (43 bytes); per-node counter state confirmed. Matches the owner's chosen T2 fix (server-identity binding).

findings.title: DNS-rebinding TOCTOU: guard resolves DNS independently of reqwest's own resolution
findings.description: resolve_guard (line 992) performs its own DNS resolution via (host, port).to_socket_addrs() (999) and inspects those IPs, but the actual fetch in http_get_bytes (609) lets reqwest resolve the hostname a second time. No shared/pinned resolution between check and use. An attacker-controlled authoritative DNS with near-zero TTL answers the guard's lookup with a public IP (passes) and reqwest's subsequent lookup with 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 / RFC1918. Applies to web_fetch, package download (resolved.url), and registry queries.
findings.exploit_scenario: Client submits http://rebind.attacker.com/. Attacker DNS returns 203.0.113.10 for the guard, then flips to 169.254.169.254 (TTL 0). When the worker runs the fetch, reqwest re-resolves and connects to the metadata service; the response (credentials) is spooled and downloaded.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the host once in the guard, validate the IP, then force reqwest to connect to that exact IP (Client::builder().resolve(host, addr) or a custom connector). Reject hosts with multiple A records unless all pass.
findings.confidence_reason: Two independent resolutions (guard vs reqwest) confirmed in source; classic rebinding window. Confirms T1's TOCTOU sub-case.

findings.title: Pre-auth rate-limit is keyed on the spoofable source IP, allowing the SPA gate to be fed without limit
findings.description: rate_limit_allows (line 191) keys the token bucket on src_ip (199), read straight from the attacker-controlled IPv4 header (75). For a UDP SPA datagram, an attacker spoofing a different source IP per packet gets a fresh full bucket every time, so rate_limit_allows returns true and emit_spa_event pushes the packet to the userspace Ed25519 verifier (142-153). The per-IP bucket imposes no aggregate limit on spoofed traffic reaching the expensive verification pipeline; RATE_LIMIT (16384 entries) also fills with attacker keys. Matches T3 (per-IP rate-limit defeated by source-IP spoofing); reported as a structural control bypass, not plain volumetric DoS.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker sends UDP datagrams to the SPA port with structurally valid SPA headers (version 0x47 0x50, reserved flags clear) and a randomized spoofed src_ip per packet. Every packet allocates a new full bucket and passes, flooding SPA_RING and the userspace verifier with signature checks while never being rate-limited.
findings.recommendation: Add a global SPA verification budget (a single global token bucket in addition to the per-IP one) so spoofed-source floods are bounded regardless of src_ip diversity; bound/evict RATE_LIMIT growth.
findings.confidence_reason: Token bucket keyed on raw header src_ip confirmed; fresh bucket per new key. In scope as a spoofing-based control bypass per the threat model.

findings.title: static_path follows symlinks out of webroot — no canonicalization or containment check
findings.description: static_path (118-135) builds the served path by pushing each URI component onto webroot. It correctly rejects non-Normal components (130) so .. / absolute / Windows-prefix traversal is blocked, but it never canonicalizes or verifies the final path is contained within webroot. static_response then does fs::read(&path) (105-106) and returns the bytes. If any symlink exists under webroot (e.g. webroot/data -> /etc), an unauthenticated client requesting /data/passwd resolves the symlink at the FS layer and discloses a file outside webroot. Attacker controls the URI path entirely; the only precondition is a symlink under the configured webroot, common in real deployments (e.g. release 'current' links). This is T6.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operator's webroot contains a symlink (a 'latest' build link or leftover). An internet client sends GET /latest/secret.key where webroot/latest -> a deploy dir outside webroot, or GET /backup where webroot/backup -> /var/backups. fs::read follows the link and returns out-of-webroot content with 200; content_type defaults to application/octet-stream.
findings.recommendation: Canonicalize the candidate path and the webroot once at startup, then verify the resolved path starts_with the canonical webroot before reading; reject on canonicalize failure or escape. Consider O_NOFOLLOW for the final component. Add a symlink fixture test.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified no canonicalize/containment check anywhere; path built purely by component push. Realistic only when a symlink exists under webroot, hence MEDIUM.

findings.title: Missing counter-state file silently resets all replay counters to zero
findings.description: load_counter_state treats a NotFound file as a clean empty map (line 159 returns Ok(HashMap::new())). In verify the per-key highest counter then defaults to 0 (125-129), so after the state file is lost the first replayed packet with any counter >=1 passes counter <= highest_counter (130) and is accepted. Nothing detects the regression or refuses to start. Corrupt/partial TOML is handled differently (163-164 propagate an error -> startup fails, the safe behavior); the gap is specifically the missing-file case and any operation that truncates the file. This is T4.
findings.exploit_scenario: Counter file is deleted/lost (disk wipe, failed deploy, container without persistent volume, cleanup script, operator error). An attacker who previously captured any valid SPA (counter=N) replays it; highest_counter is now 0 so it is accepted while inside the window. Every old captured SPA up to the client's true current counter becomes replayable until the client advances past it.
findings.recommendation: On a missing state file in production, fail closed (refuse to start or require an explicit init flag) rather than starting with empty counters. Persist with fsync, consider a monotonic floor / signed watermark, and treat any counter regression as a hard error.
findings.confidence_reason: NotFound -> empty map -> default 0 confirmed; distinct from the corrupt-file path which correctly errors.

findings.title: emit_spa_event leaves the tail of the SpaEvent payload uninitialized in the ring buffer
findings.description: SPA_RING.reserve::<SpaEvent>(0) (231) hands back 136 uninitialized bytes. The writer sets src_ip (0-3), src_port (4-5), payload_len (6-7) and copies indices 0..=106 into payload at offsets 8..=114 (107 bytes). The payload field spans offsets 8..=135, so offsets 115..=135 (last 21 payload bytes) are never written and retain prior ring-buffer memory. Userspace parse_spa_event (ghost-proxy-server/src/ebpf.rs:388) read_unaligned's the whole 136-byte struct. Disclosure is currently contained only because payload_len is hardcoded to 107 (238) and userspace slices payload[..payload_len] (ebpf.rs:146), so the tail is never consumed. If payload_len ever becomes length-driven (see F-010) or a consumer reads the whole array, this becomes kernel-memory disclosure to userspace/logs.
findings.exploit_scenario: No direct network exploit today (payload_len pinned to 107). Latent: any change making payload_len reflect the real attacker-controlled UDP length up to 128 exposes 21 bytes of uninitialized kernel ring-buffer memory per event.
findings.recommendation: Zero the reservation before writing (memset the full payload range) so no uninitialized bytes can ever be submitted regardless of payload_len semantics.
findings.confidence_reason: Byte-offset accounting verified; latent today but a clear uninitialized-memory hazard coupled to F-010.

findings.title: Package digest verification silently skipped when registry omits a digest (ExpectedDigest::None)
findings.description: ExpectedDigest::None.verify() returns Ok(()) unconditionally (772). All three resolvers fall back to None when the digest field is missing: pypi unwrap_or(None) (878), npm (913, also requires SRI to be a parseable sha512 — a sha256/sha1-only integrity yields None), crates (938). Combined with the registry-controlled URL (F-012) and redirect/rebinding (F-002/F-004), an attacker who can influence the registry response or the fetched content can deliver arbitrary bytes that pass 'verification' because no digest was set. Undermines the T7 control 'digest verification for packages.'
findings.exploit_scenario: A malicious/MITM'd registry response omits digests.sha256 (pypi) or supplies only a non-sha512 integrity (npm), yielding None. The attacker then serves arbitrary tarball/wheel bytes (via controlled URL or redirect); verify passes, and the poisoned package is spooled and delivered to the client as a trusted artifact.
findings.recommendation: Treat a missing/unsupported digest as a hard failure for package downloads (bail in resolve when no usable digest is present), or require TLS host pinning to the official CDN before accepting a None digest. At minimum log and refuse rather than silently accept.
findings.confidence_reason: None-path returns Ok unconditionally and all resolvers have a None fallback; depends on registry/MITM influence so MEDIUM.

findings.title: Kernel hardcodes SpaEvent.payload_len to SPA_MIN_LEN and copies a fixed 107 bytes, ignoring actual length
findings.description: emit_spa_event always writes payload_len = SPA_MIN_LEN (238) and copy_payload_bytes! always copies a fixed 107-byte window (240-256), using the literal SPA_MIN_LEN as the copy limit rather than the actual payload_len argument (224). Any SPA padding (bytes 107..=127, protocol-supported up to 128) is dropped, and userspace is told every event is 107 bytes. Safe for verification today because the signature only covers the first 43 bytes and padding is unsigned. But the writer/reader contract is wrong: payload_len does not describe the datagram, the per-byte copy ignores its parameter, and the payload_len > SPA_MAX_LEN guard in userspace (ebpf.rs:141) is dead code. Couples to F-008.
findings.exploit_scenario: No memory corruption today. Contract drift: a maintainer who wires payload_len to the real length, or relies on padding delivery, gets truncated data or (with F-008) uninitialized-memory disclosure.
findings.recommendation: Clamp payload_len to min(payload_len, SPA_MAX_LEN), write that value, copy exactly that many bytes (with existing per-byte bounds checks), and zero the remainder.
findings.confidence_reason: Hardcoded length and fixed-window copy confirmed; latent contract issue, hence LOW.

findings.title: Git bundle read into memory with no size cap; max_object_len enforced only after full allocation
findings.description: In run_git_mirror the attacker-controlled remote is cloned with git clone --mirror, bundled, then the entire bundle is slurped via fs::read(&bundle) (571) with no size limit. The guard outcome.body.len() > max_object_len runs in process_job_inner (478) AFTER the whole bundle is in RAM as one Vec<u8>. Unlike the HTTP path (http_get_bytes, 619-627) which checks content_length and streamed length against max_len, the git path has no streaming cap and no on-disk size check before reading. The clone is bounded only by git_timeout (120s default), during which a malicious/large remote delivers gigabytes to disk; the subsequent fs::read allocates all of it. An unbounded single-allocation/OOM primitive that bypasses the ordering of max_object_len.
findings.exploit_scenario: Client submits SUBMIT_GIT pointing at an attacker git server (or a known-huge repo). Within the timeout the relay clones and bundles a multi-GB repo. fs::read allocates the full bundle; with 4 default workers several jobs run concurrently, multiplying resident memory and OOM-killing the proxy.
findings.recommendation: Before fs::read, stat the bundle and bail if it exceeds max_object_len. Cap the on-disk clone (--depth/--filter or post-clone size check) and read through a length-limited reader (take(max_object_len)).
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed fs::read with size check only afterward; bounded by timeout not size. Memory-amplification primitive, MEDIUM.

findings.title: Registry-controlled download URL enables fetch redirection (crates dl_path concatenation, pypi/npm absolute URLs)
findings.description: Download URLs come from untrusted registry JSON. resolve_pypi takes chosen.url verbatim (870), resolve_npm takes dist.tarball verbatim (904), and resolve_crates does string concatenation format!("https://crates.io{dl_path}") (940) where dl_path is registry-controlled. The crates concatenation is dangerous: a dl_path like @evil.com/x produces https://crates.io@evil.com/x (userinfo trick -> host becomes evil.com). validate_public_url is re-run on resolved.url (587) but is still subject to the redirect (F-002) and rebinding (F-004) bypasses, and the @ host-confusion means the guard validates evil.com rather than crates.io.
findings.exploit_scenario: A compromised/malicious upstream registry or a MITM on the registry response returns dl_path: @169.254.169.254.nip.io/x or a tarball URL pointing at an attacker host that 302-redirects internally. The relay fetches the attacker-chosen host and spools the response, turning the package path into a second SSRF vector with weaker guarantees than direct web_fetch.
findings.recommendation: Do not string-concatenate registry paths onto a base host; parse dl_path as a path-only relative reference and join via Url::join against a fixed crates.io base, asserting the resulting host equals the expected CDN host. For pypi/npm, allowlist the artifact host against known CDN domains before fetching.
findings.confidence_reason: String concat + verbatim registry URLs confirmed; the @-userinfo host-confusion is real. Requires registry/MITM influence, MEDIUM.

findings.title: Non-IPv4 (incl. IPv6) traffic returns XDP_PASS, bypassing the proxy-port gate over IPv6
findings.description: try_ghost_proxy_xdp returns XDP_PASS for any ethertype that is not IPv4 (60-62), and the catch-all error path also PASSes (50). An IPv6 packet to the proxy port (TCP 8443) is never matched by handle_tcp (IPv4 only, 79) and is passed through, so the 'drop unless src_ip in allow-map' concealment property does not hold over IPv6 — the proxy port is reachable/probeable via IPv6 regardless of allow-map state. Documented and explicitly risk-accepted in THREAT_MODEL §5 (IPv4-only deployment), reported LOW for completeness; material if the fleet goes dual-stack.
findings.exploit_scenario: On a dual-stack host, an observer connects to [host]:8443 over IPv6 and reaches the Noise/proxy listener (or observes it respond), defeating the IPv4 XDP concealment.
findings.recommendation: When deploying dual-stack, extend the XDP program to parse IPv6 + extension headers and apply the same allow-map/drop logic, or default-drop non-IPv4 to the proxy port at the gate.
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed non-IPv4 PASS; owner risk-accepted for the IPv4-only deployment, so LOW.

findings.title: Check-then-write race lets concurrent jobs exceed per-client storage/job quotas
findings.description: Storage quota is enforced by read-then-write with no locking in two racing places: (1) post-fetch in process_job_inner 473-489 — client_usage() reads existing_bytes, checks existing_bytes + body.len() > max_bytes_per_client, then fs::write persists; (2) pre-check in enqueue 255-294. With worker_count workers (default 4) and multiple concurrent tunnels for the same key_id, several jobs read the same pre-write snapshot, all pass, then all write. No lock/transaction (client_usage just walks the jobs dir). The byte-quota race combined with F-011 (each object up to max_object_len) lets on-disk total exceed max_bytes_per_client by up to ~worker_count * max_object_len.
findings.exploit_scenario: Client fills storage just under quota, then submits N git/web jobs near-simultaneously. All N workers call client_usage() before any writes, each sees bytes below the limit, all pass, all write, pushing stored bytes well past max_bytes_per_client.
findings.recommendation: Serialize quota accounting per client_dir (a per-key async mutex held across the usage read + write), or keep an atomic reserved-bytes counter incremented at reservation and reconciled on completion/failure so check and commit are atomic.
findings.confidence_reason: Unsynchronized read-then-write across a worker pool confirmed; bounded over-quota write rather than transient pressure, MEDIUM.

findings.title: SRI parser does not validate decoded SHA-512 length, coercing malformed integrity instead of rejecting
findings.description: parse_sri_sha512 (962) strips the sha512- prefix and base64-decodes, returning whatever bytes result without asserting a 64-byte length. The vector is stored as ExpectedDigest::Sha512(expected) and compared by actual.as_slice() == expected.as_slice() (787). A short/malformed payload makes the comparison always mismatch (fails closed -> targeted unavailability for that package), and malformed SRI is silently coerced rather than rejected, masking which algorithm is enforced. Not a direct bypass (mismatch fails closed) but the missing length check is a robustness/availability and auditing gap.
findings.exploit_scenario: A malicious registry returns integrity: sha512-AAAA. parse_sri_sha512 returns a 3-byte vec; verify always mismatches. Legit clients can never retrieve that package (targeted DoS), and the relay never warns the integrity field was malformed.
findings.recommendation: After base64-decode, require decoded.len() == 64 before constructing ExpectedDigest::Sha512; otherwise return None or bail explicitly so malformed integrity is distinguishable from a genuine mismatch.
findings.confidence_reason: Missing length check confirmed; fails closed so impact is availability/robustness, LOW.

findings.title: Tier check precedes signature verification, creating an unauthenticated key-enumeration / tier oracle
findings.description: In SpaVerifier::verify the order is parse -> look up client by key_id (110) -> reject unknown key (111) -> reject non-Full tier (114-116) -> only then verify the Ed25519 signature (118). Because key_id is the first 8 bytes of SHA-256 of a client public key (low-entropy/discoverable), an unauthenticated attacker supplying a guessed/known key_id with a garbage signature receives distinguishable outcomes before any crypto check: UnknownKey vs UnauthorizedTier vs (real key) BadSignature. This leaks roster membership and per-member tier without any private key, and the distinct error variants can surface as distinguishable responses/timing on the HTTPS-SPA endpoint. Does not open the gate (signature still gates acceptance), hence LOW, but erodes the high-sensitivity roster asset.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker iterates candidate key_ids against the HTTPS-SPA endpoint with dummy signatures. Differing responses/timing for UnknownKey vs UnauthorizedTier vs BadSignature confirm which key_ids are enrolled and which tier they hold, mapping the roster of a concealed service.
findings.recommendation: Verify the signature before the tier/authorization decision, and collapse all pre-acceptance failures into a single indistinguishable rejection (same error, no per-reason response/timing variation) on the network-facing path.
findings.confidence_reason: Check ordering confirmed; oracle strength depends on observable response/timing differences, so LOW.

findings.title: Registry JSON queries (get_json) bypass resolve_guard and follow redirects
findings.description: resolve_pypi/resolve_npm/resolve_crates call get_json(http, &url) (857, 887, 926) which does http.get(url).send() (947) with no resolve_guard and the same redirect-following client. Base URLs are hard-coded to pypi.org/npmjs.org/crates.io so the host is not directly attacker-chosen, but name/version are interpolated into the path (validate_package_name permits / @ .), and these registries can 3xx-redirect to attacker/internal hosts with no per-hop guard. Lower severity because the immediate host is fixed; another unguarded sink that trusts upstream redirects and compounds F-002/F-004.
findings.exploit_scenario: A crafted name path segment plus an upstream/MITM redirect on the registry response causes get_json to follow a Location into an internal host; the JSON body/error is processed server-side. Primarily a defense-in-depth gap once a redirect is in play.
findings.recommendation: Route get_json through the same hardened client (per-hop resolve_guard, IP pinning) and assert the final connected host is the expected registry domain.
findings.confidence_reason: Unguarded get_json with redirects confirmed; fixed base host limits direct attacker control, LOW.

findings.title: Client-static verification uses a non-constant-time comparison
findings.description: verify_client_noise_static (176-183) compares the initiator's static key against the SPA-authorized key with remote_static != expected.as_slice() (179), a short-circuiting byte comparison rather than constant-time equality. The compared value is the client's Noise public static key (not a server secret) recovered from an already-AEAD-authenticated IK message 1 (snow read_message at 101 succeeds first), so leakage is limited and the attacker must already be a valid post-SPA peer. Flagged because this is the core tunnel-auth gate. Verification ordering itself is correct: this check (103) runs after message-1 decryption and before message 2 (108), transport mode (110), and any dispatch (119-137).
findings.exploit_scenario: A post-SPA-but-unauthorized peer (e.g. via T2 cross-node replay) repeatedly handshakes and measures timing to learn leading bytes of an authorized client's public static key. Public-key recovery yields little on its own, so defense-in-depth.
findings.recommendation: Use a constant-time comparison (subtle::ConstantTimeEq / constant_time_eq) for the static-key check.
findings.confidence_reason: Non-constant-time compare confirmed but on a public key behind decryption; minimal real impact, LOW.

findings.title: No '--' end-of-options separator before client URL in git clone (defense-in-depth gap)
findings.description: run_git_mirror builds git clone --mirror --quiet <url> <mirror> passing the raw client string metadata.source as a positional arg (553) with no -- separator. Option-smuggling via a leading - is currently prevented because validate_git_url requires reqwest::Url::parse with an http/https/git scheme, so the string must begin with a scheme and cannot start with -. Residual risk: this relies entirely on the parser invariant — any future relaxation of the scheme check would become exploitable with no second line of defense. git:// is also accepted (unauthenticated cleartext transport, mitigated by resolve_guard). The bundle command (560-568) does not set GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT/GIT_ASKPASS, and neither command pins protocol.allow / GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM.
findings.exploit_scenario: No working exploit against current code (every accepted URL starts with a scheme prefix so it cannot be read as a flag). If scheme validation is loosened or bypassed, the missing -- lets a --prefixed value be interpreted by git as an option (e.g. --upload-pack).
findings.recommendation: Insert .arg("--") before .arg(&metadata.source). Set GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0/GIT_ASKPASS=true on the bundle command too, and consider GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1 + HOME isolation and protocol restrictions so system/global gitconfig and credential helpers cannot influence the clone.
findings.confidence_reason: Not exploitable today given the scheme invariant; pure defense-in-depth, LOW.

findings.title: copy_payload_byte uses wrapping_add for its bounds check instead of checked_add
findings.description: In copy_payload_byte, after computing ptr = data + payload_offset + INDEX via checked_add (317-320), the per-byte end check is let end = ptr.wrapping_add(1); if end <= data_end (321-322). If ptr were usize::MAX, wrapping_add(1) yields 0 and 0 <= data_end passes, permitting a read at usize::MAX. In practice the preceding checked_add chain would have to land exactly on usize::MAX for a real kernel data pointer plus a payload offset/index bounded by SPA_MIN_LEN, which is not realistically reachable. Noted because the sibling accessors read_u8/read_be_u16/read_be_u32 correctly use checked_add(1) plus a strict > comparison (282-288), making this the lone inconsistent bounds check.
findings.exploit_scenario: Not realistically exploitable: requires the kernel packet data pointer plus a small bounded offset to equal usize::MAX. Reported for defense-in-depth consistency.
findings.recommendation: Mirror read_u8: use ptr.checked_add(1).ok_or(())? (or skip on overflow) and a strict > comparison rather than wrapping_add.
findings.confidence_reason: Inconsistency is real but effectively unreachable given pointer arithmetic constraints, lowest confidence.
